skill.name: context-management
skill.description: Context management guidance for multi-agent AI workflows, long-running tasks, session handoffs, and persistent project memory.
skill.summary: Preserve, compress, and hand off project context across AI sessions.
skill.author: Joseph OBrien

security_audit.summary: Static external command, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance alerts are false positives caused by markdown fences, memory-tool examples, relationship labels, and ordinary documentation text. The confirmed risk is semantic: the memory workflow recommends preserving API endpoints and credentials in persistent memory, and a temporary-file artifact pattern could expose sensitive agent context if copied directly.

medium_findings.title: Persistent Credential Storage Guidance
medium_findings.description: The memory workflow lists API endpoints and credentials as information to preserve. Storing credentials in persistent memory can expose secrets across sessions, users, or tools unless explicit redaction and access controls are applied.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file directly names credentials as information to preserve, which confirms a real secret-retention risk. A later caution about sensitive information reduces confidence that the intent is malicious.

low_findings.title: Temporary Context Artifact Pattern
low_findings.description: The reference pattern writes subagent output to predictable files under /tmp and reads matching artifacts. This is documentation rather than executable code, but copied workflows could leak sensitive context or read unrelated files on shared systems.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is shown as a Python example and is not executed by the skill. The location and glob pattern are still specific enough to warrant a low-risk publishing warning.

content.user_title: Manage Context Across Long AI Workflows
content.value_statement: Long AI projects lose important decisions when work moves across sessions or agents. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured handoff, memory, and compression patterns.
actual_capabilities: Defines when to capture, compress, distribute, and archive project context.
Provides formats for quick context, full context, and archived context summaries.
Describes memory graph workflows for projects, preferences, entities, and relationships.
Includes handoff templates for completed tasks and blocked work.
Offers patterns for coordinating multiple agents with shared and agent-specific context.
limitations: It is documentation guidance and does not include executable automation.
Persistent memory storage depends on available memory or MCP tooling.
Sensitive data must be redacted before storage or handoff.
The templates need adaptation for each project and team workflow.

use_cases.title: Resume Long Projects
use_cases.description: Capture goals, decisions, blockers, and next steps so work can continue after a session break.

use_cases.title: Coordinate Agent Teams
use_cases.description: Prepare shared and role-specific context for reviewers, implementers, testers, or documentation agents.

use_cases.title: Maintain Project Memory
use_cases.description: Organize preferences, architecture choices, and relationships in a memory graph for future retrieval.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Handoff
prompt_templates.prompt: Capture the current task, completed work, open questions, blockers, and next recommended action in a concise handoff.

prompt_templates.title: Compress Project Context
prompt_templates.prompt: Compress this project context into a quick version under 500 tokens while preserving decisions, constraints, and active risks.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Agent Context
prompt_templates.prompt: Create shared context and separate instructions for implementation, test, review, and documentation agents.

prompt_templates.title: Design Memory Graph Updates
prompt_templates.prompt: Identify entities, observations, and relationships to store for this project. Exclude secrets and mark uncertain facts clearly.

output_examples.input: The user finished a refactor and needs the next agent to write tests.
output: Completed work: refactor scope and changed modules.
Important decisions: chosen interfaces and compatibility constraints.
Next agent focus: add regression tests and verify affected workflows.

output_examples.input: A project has too much chat history to pass into a new session.
output: A quick context summary with current goal, constraints, and blockers.
A full context summary with architecture, decisions, and integration points.
An archive note for historical decisions that are not needed immediately.

output_examples.input: A team wants persistent memory for preferences and project decisions.
output: Suggested project, user, technology, and decision entities.
Observation notes with source and date guidance.
Relationship labels that connect projects, tools, issues, and decisions.

best_practices: Capture rationale with each important decision, not only the final outcome.
Redact credentials, tokens, private keys, and personal data before storing context.
Keep handoffs short, current, and targeted to the next agent or session.
anti_patterns: Do not store raw secrets in persistent memory or handoff files.
Do not pass complete chat history when a concise summary is enough.
Do not create many low-value relationships that make memory harder to search.

faq.question: Does this skill run code or commands?
faq.answer: No. It provides markdown guidance, templates, and workflow patterns for context management.

faq.question: Can it preserve context across sessions?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes checkpoints, memory graph entries, and handoff summaries for session continuity.

faq.question: Does it require a memory MCP server?
faq.answer: Some memory workflows assume memory MCP tools, but the handoff and compression templates can be used without them.

faq.question: Is it safe to store credentials with this skill?
faq.answer: No. Credentials and secrets should be redacted before any persistent memory or file-based handoff is created.

faq.question: How does it help multi-agent workflows?
faq.answer: It separates shared project context from role-specific context so each agent receives relevant information.

faq.question: What tools does it support?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
